text: Hi there.
Hey, Holly, how are you?
Good, good.
Thank you.

So I appreciate the flexibility.
Oh, good.

It's missing.
And I'm like, what do you mean?
We just pay seven to refill the booth.
It's like, yeah, the people delivering it don't know where it is and lost the tracking number.
How often does that happen?
That's so crazy.
Never happened.
Like even the vendor that did this all was like, never happened to me.
It got found the next day or something weird, but it was.
It's like pretty, pretty nice.
Yeah, that's, that's not very helpful.
But, but awesome to, awesome to connect.

We'll love to maybe like, I know you have an amazing background and super familiar with, with Outreach, but we're always awesome to hear a little bit of background, just any additional context that would be helpful and then happy to do intro on my end as well.
Yeah, that's great.
So I don't, we'll probably have more questions as we get into things, but, so I just joined Outreach in December.
I've been in B2B marketing and I also did B2C in a prior life and ran my own business and worked in startups and worked in big companies.
So I've kind of, you know, been around the block a little bit with respect to marketing.
I, I came to Outreach for a couple of reasons.
One, it's a really good market place to be because there's so much opportunity and growth.
So that was one thing.
Second thing is I have.
Mainly, I've done a lot of time working in CX, so selling to sales, service, marketing, commerce, buyers.
And I actually went, my last job, I was doing GRC, and it just wasn't as interesting and fun as trying to focus on kind of customer-facing sorts of software.
So that's another reason.
And then I worked with Abhijit, our CEO at ServiceNow.
And so one of the things we did there, we basically started in a business unit for the company that was focused on selling to customer service buyers.
So the company had traditionally been an IT company or focused on IT buyers, lower down the food chain, and eventually move their way up the food chain.
But they had never sold to a line of business like customer service and HR and things like that.
And so Abhijit and I started the customer service business.
I was the first marketing hire.
He was the product engineering leader.
And we built that business into about $400 million business in a few years.
Did you cross paths with Voskin?
What's that?
Did you cross paths with Voskin when you were servicing?
Voskin was there too, yeah.

He's a friend.

Yeah.

December.

so, and then now obviously like Karan is our lead investor and the, you know, obviously an investor.

so, so a lot of connections with Outreach.

so I'm a more technical CMO, if you will, right?

I know it's a little early of you in your tenure, right?
Like, but I'm sure you have some thoughts as well as to why, you know, this conversation came about as well.
Yeah, so, you know, it's funny.
There are a lot of different things to analyze, but I think one of...

One biggest things is the, and you probably see this because I'm sure you've worked with some other companies who are our size or smaller, but if I just think about some of our goals, the company started off really focusing on almost more of the SMB mid-market segments, and yet we have a lot of large enterprise customers as well, so SAP, Siemens, MX, and so on.

And one of the things that we're focused on right now is going from, we'll still continue to do mid-market where it makes sense, probably not doing as much small business, and then moving and really focusing on enterprise.
So that's a big area of focus for us.

And so in doing that, you know, there are a lot of, it's not just like processes internally, that's one component, but it's also getting the right kind of marketing and being diverse and being in the right watering holes.
And all of that.
For that audience, you know, you have to have more sophisticated programs.
have to have higher touch activities like ABM and VIP events and things like that because it is a different audience.
So I'd say that's the first thing.
The second thing is that because the product started off as being an XDR product, BDR, SDR, whatever you want to call it, that's who we were selling to, which at the lower down in the organization.
We weren't selling to CROs as much.
And we do have a lot of those contacts, but the conversations are different.
And going and selling a platform solution that serves different audiences in the organization and connects all your revenue points is different than just selling to an XDR.
So we're also trying to move to that platform story where we can tell the right story for a CRO, head of rev ops.
And And And
And CMO and even CIO.
So that's newer for the company.
And then we mainly sell most of our businesses in the U.S., but we do sell in EMEA as well.
So I'd say that's our second focus.
And we're going to amp things up a little bit there.
Or, you know, it's not going to be at the level of the U.S., but we have an opportunity to do some cool things there.
What else am I forgetting?
We've also, I guess the rest of this sort of relates.
We're also picking up tastes on awareness a little bit.
And, you know, this is always a challenge because there were some, I think like most companies over the last couple of years, you know, it's like the pandemic and then post-pandemic.
There have been a lot of layoffs and a lot of budget cuts, not just in marketing, but elsewhere.
marketing always gets hit hard because marketing.
You know, big dollars in front of it.
And so there were some more cross-air measures put in place with Outreach.
And I don't want to say that's been relaxed, but we were doing some very intentional investment.
And one of the areas is to also make sure we have some dollars allocated to awareness as well.
And not just hitting pipe numbers.
And it's interesting because, you know, especially dealing with the executive team and board members, Karan, I think, probably understands this.
And we have another person on the board who was actually a marketing operator, you know, so had the experience.
But most of the people on the board were never marketers.
So they don't necessarily understand the difference between awareness and demand generation activities and expectations and what you get out of it.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
Well, that's super helpful.
And I feel like I know really well, because like I said, like I was the buyer.
I was also the user.
And I also.

I've done A to Z, essentially, so I'm super, super familiar.

Okay, cool.
That usually happens sometimes.
Sometimes, hey, we had a whole conversation, and then, I mean, the intro, sometimes, like, there's a one-liner, and that's all I'm going by, you know, but...
Yeah, and by the way, also, didn't...
Bruno's here as well.
We didn't...
Yeah, Bruno, you want to do a quick intro as well?
Yeah, definitely.
I am the wannabe buyer, so I've been in sales, B2B, for now the last 15 years, coming from oil and gas, right?
And Marcel's my brother.
Um...
Yeah,
You launched the company last year.
We've been talking about maybe helping out some and primarily coming in to learn.
But I've been the guy that I've been trying to get with Baker Hughes, for example, my last company I worked for, trying to get Outreach implemented.
And now still to record everything in Teams and then go manually and scrub the calls for insights.
So it was just horrible.
It was painful working without your product.
Hopefully, we can get in a partnership here now and promote the heck out of you guys.
I like that.
Yeah, well, thanks for that, And it's super high level, too.
It's like I've been in a bunch of different companies, CMO and leading growth and marketing.
And my co-founder, CTO, Daniel, he was head of engineering at a company called IFTTT.
I don't know if you remember IFTTT.
Yeah, of course.
And so a lot of workflows, millions of users.
But I think most of my career, like the main thing I've done is just think through, like, how can we think more like publishers?
Brands become more like publishers.
And because publishers are always earning people's trust and attention, if you will, by helping them and being useful.
And that's kind of what I built my whole career.
And I built a site at IBM called secretintelligence.com.
And it was the first content hub before content marketing was even a term.
And then after won the best B2B marketing site award and had generated 17 million in revenue in the first year, we're like, oh, this kind of works.
It's way better than ads.
There's compounding results.
And I know Outreach gets it because I think early on, you all were very early in even acquiring, I think, saleshacker.com or other publications.
And so definitely there's huge kind of traction there.

But as I built this throughout my career, when I was a deep program, I just started taking everything I had learned in building these publications and building these kind of organic content engines and essentially building workflows, AI workflows to distill a lot of the knowledge.
And processes that we had, right?
And then hiring the editors that I normally would hire, but put them in the loop and sort of do that.
And things kind of took off.
We published thousands of pages and launched a newsletter called aiminds.com, which almost 300,000 subscribers, all of that in like six months, essentially.
And Karan was on the board of Dgram.
So he's kind of saw firsthand how it worked.
And so it's always easy to look at this and go, okay, that's easy, right?
But as I did that, I wanted to just start teaching people and showing exactly what I did.
So I started hosting these paid workshops.
They're like full day, $500 to $1,000 per person.
And people paid and came to it.
But then at the end of these workshops, a lot of people said, hey, I don't want you to just build these workflows and adapt them to me.
I actually want the strategy.
And I want the actual whole team as a service to execute it week in and week out and experiment and try new things, right?
And we started to do it for a lot of companies and started to see huge growth.
You know, we started to get a lot of like huge logos like Ramp and Reddit and others to kind of sign up and then kind of things took off from there.
And we, you know, happy to say that we closed our Series A about a month ago, but we actually grew from zero to six million in ARR, all bootstrapped and profitable.
And so, you know, it was like a huge pride and validation thing, you know, like not having to raise a single dollar to profitability and growth, you know.
And so, but a lot of the kind of like what we built here is just like focusing more on outputs, right, and outcomes.
And so I think part of the issue, and you've probably seen this a bunch, is, you know, people don't want to hire massive teams anymore, usually, or at least that's what we're hearing.
There's this kind of urgency of moving fast.
Pay channels are becoming less and less efficient and effective, right?
And there's a lot of AI tools, but there's, you know, it puts all the burden on people to do a lot of the work, right?
And so, so kind of what we do.
So is really just focus more on the outcomes and build this kind of content, if you will, engine, right?
And I'll show you like the actual like behind the scenes of kind of how we do it.
But essentially the way our teams are set up is, think of it as like, it looks like an agency, but it's kind of like a best class agency with a ton of tech underneath, right?
And so for instance, like our chief content officer, Matthew, he was the editor in chief at TechCrunch for 10 years.
So he ran TechCrunch for 10 years.
A lot of our folks on our team, like they're the people behind a lot of the massive growths you've seen.
Like, so for instance, like Kirkland was a ClickUp.
We have Dave built Fanatics from the ground up to 600 million a year.
You know, Stuart built NerdWallet and was at NerdWallet.
And so a lot of these kind of like examples you see out there of sites that have grown to hundreds of millions of users.
Like we essentially hire the best of the best to...
Kind of oversee a lot of these programs that we manage for customers.
so ultimately, like what we're doing is building a organic content engine as a service, right?
And so like most of the engagements that we're doing, like with, let's say like Superhuman, Reddit, Ramp, a lot of it starts with editorial and content refresh and also programmatic SEO, right?

And so just to give you like a little bit of the behind the scenes, like a lot of what we build is this workflow engine that experts build, but it's represented in code and a runtime layer to run it, right?

Like, so for instance, like this is a workflow where the input was just like website and it's generating a brief understanding like you're based on your website, everything there is to know about your company, your product, you know, the different personas, their jobs to be done.
And then generating simple queries and starting to execute against that, you know, and so it's pretty cool.
Because we essentially have these workflows operating at a massive, massive scale.
And that's kind of how we go about doing everything from ideation to publishing content and optimizing every single week, right?
And so just to give you a little bit of an idea, like some of our workflows and kind of like, you know, this looks a little bit more complex.
But the idea is like, if you think about kind of agentic workflows, it's this, but on steroids with our runtime layer.
And then like just one last thing I'll mention, then I'll shut up and kind of we can dig in.
But essentially, like the way to think about it is we distill how experts would go about publishing content and creating content and doing research and turn it into detailed workflows.
But then inserted those experts back into the loop to get that process along.
So that we can deliver, you know, a lot higher quality outputs and scale essentially, right?
So think of it as like publishing a hundred thousand.
Words, but at 10 to 20 cents a word instead of dollars a word, you know, but with the same quality and higher quality and faster output.
So that's a little bit of what we've done.
And it's kind of really interesting, right?
Like, because I was kind of digging in a little bit and you all have done like so, so much content.
But certainly, like, it's really interesting to see kind of like, especially as you see some of your mindshare competitors, if you will, right?
Like, not that they compete directly with you, but certainly, like, they in a very short period of time have been able to essentially like 5 to 10x your traffic, you know, pretty quickly.
And then they're going to continue to gain that mindshare, right?
And so that's kind of the same challenge as superhumans having right now.
They're awesome, but they're losing mindshare to Notion, and Notion now is competing with them with Notion Mail, you know?
So they're yeah.
But anyways, I'll shut up here, but hopefully it's helpful.
As far as just like context and background.
No, it's great.
So maybe like, how would we go about doing like a project?
Yeah.
So the way we work is everything I would do in the first 12 months of joining a company and building these, like we try to do in an accelerated way, right?
Like, so the way to think about it is organic growth is like an infinite gain.
And so it's not like you publish a few articles and then you call it a day.
It's mostly like crafting the strategy and then continue to iterate day in and day out, right?
Like, and so what we do is we almost go through this like 12 week program as part of the initial engagement that then from there we continue to iterate.
And so the idea is like crafting the strategy and then calibrating on style, tone, voice, and then starting to publish and then starting to look at those signals and iterating every single week.
And so that's kind of how it looks for a lot of customers.
of customers.

It's interesting.
We get that from our analytics team.

But that's ultimately it, right?
Like, right now you have about...
Like 400 pages, you know, let's call it 10 of those 400 are generating most of the traffic, right?
And there's like just within that, there's like a massive, massive opportunity.
But the way we go about it is like we approach it from a couple of different angles, like to develop the strategy.
So one angle is like the personas you care about and that you're going after and then understanding their jobs to be done.
And what are the things that they're trying to do essentially, right?
And then the other part is like looking at your product and then understanding like the pain points that it solves and the things that it enables.
And then the last part is looking at Mindshare competitors and what are they ranking for?
And so think of those as like three different circles and there's some overlap between those three.
And then based on that, we're working with your teams to essentially calibrate and understand like where do we want to play some bets?
But the difference is instead of doing like two or three pieces of content a month, like we can do five, 10, potentially even more.
Or do it programmatically.

Create a lot of, like, content that's high quality and on-brand, but then be able to kind of plant a lot more seeds and learn from those a lot faster, which is a lot of, like, what Clay and others have done, you know, and how they kind of 10x their traffic.
And you're right, like, a lot of this might not be, like, can be really high quality content.
And it's not that that traffic is going to convert directly, but it's that, like, that awareness has a direct impact.
And so that's what I saw in DeepRam.
We had a 24x increase in traffic, and that created, like, a 3x increase in brand searches, but then those brand searches convert at, like, an obscene level, right?
Like, so, yeah, so that lift, it kind of, like, it follows on.
was, like, three months after a 24x in traffic, then people started searching for brand more, and then after that, we saw a massive increase in consumption and active users, you know?
And so it's kind of, like, it follows this path, if you will, you know?
But you need to, like, it's a constant game of, like, publishing high quality, you know, getting it out there.
Optimizing.

it's Gong, it's SalesLoft, Clary.

I think the challenge is you need to build a lot of topical authority around a lot of topics that even if they're not perfectly your core and what you lead with, it's going to lead to it.

And so one way to think about it is, for instance, maybe ICP is not a topic that you want to rank for or that necessarily people are searching for that are thinking of buying Outreach, but it starts to create topical authority that eventually helps raise everything up you're doing.

And I think like that's mostly kind of like a decision of like organic is a long game.
And we do do other types of work streams, like, for example, content repurposing for social and things like that.
But we tend to start with editorial just because it's what has worked so well in organic growth.
